params.text:
The psychological impact

Eating disorders are exhausting to live with. The illness can dominate a person's every waking thought, filling the mind with rules and fears about food, eating and their body. This leaves little room for anything else and can be deeply isolating, cutting people off from friends, family and the things they once enjoyed.

  • Preoccupation: thoughts about food and weight can take over.
  • Low mood: anxiety, depression and shame are common companions.
  • Isolation: people often withdraw and hide their struggle.

params.alt: Illustration for Effects on the mind

params.text:
Shame, secrecy and hope

Many people feel deep shame about their eating disorder and go to great lengths to hide it. They may deny there is a problem, resist help, or feel that they do not deserve to recover. This is part of the illness, not stubbornness, and it means your patience and warmth matter enormously.

It is vital to hold on to hope. Eating disorders are treatable, and with specialist help many people recover fully and go on to live well. The distress you see is real, but it is not permanent. By staying kind, consistent and non-judgemental, and by helping the person towards professional support, you play a genuine part in that recovery.

params.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap.
params.textField: An eating disorder can fill a person's *mind* with fears about food and cause deep *shame*, which often leads them to *hide* their struggle. Holding on to *hope* matters, because eating disorders are *treatable*.
